cards.title: Encapsulation
cards.explanation: Encapsulation bundles data (fields) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (a class), and restricts direct access to the internal state. Fields should be private; access is controlled through public getters/setters or behaviour-exposing methods. This protects invariants and lets you change the internal implementation without breaking callers.
cards.whyItMatters: Encapsulation is what makes classes trustworthy units of abstraction — callers don't need to know or care about implementation details.
cards.gotcha: Adding a public setter for every private field defeats encapsulation entirely. Only expose what callers actually need.

cards.title: Runtime Polymorphism
cards.explanation: Runtime polymorphism (dynamic dispatch) allows a reference of a supertype to point to an object of any subtype, and the correct overridden method is called at runtime based on the actual object type. In Java this is achieved through method overriding and interfaces. It is the mechanism that makes the Open/Closed Principle possible.
cards.whyItMatters: Polymorphism lets you add new types without modifying existing code — the core of extensible design.
cards.gotcha: Overloading (same method name, different parameters) is resolved at compile time based on the reference type, not the runtime type. It is NOT polymorphism and often behaves unexpectedly when combined with inheritance.

cards.title: Interface vs Abstract Class
cards.explanation: Use an interface when you want to define a contract that unrelated classes can implement (multiple interfaces are allowed). Use an abstract class when you have shared implementation or state that subclasses should inherit. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods, narrowing the gap — but abstract classes still own the 'is-a with shared behaviour' use case.
cards.whyItMatters: Choosing correctly keeps inheritance hierarchies shallow and avoids fragile base class problems.
cards.gotcha: Extending an abstract class consumes your one inheritance slot. Prefer interfaces + composition unless you genuinely need shared state or a constructor.

cards.title: Composition over Inheritance
cards.explanation: Inheritance creates a tight compile-time coupling between parent and child — changes to the parent silently break subclasses (the fragile base class problem). Composition delegates behaviour to collaborator objects held as fields, so you can swap implementations at runtime and avoid deep hierarchies. 'Favour composition over inheritance' is item 18 of Effective Java.
cards.whyItMatters: Deep inheritance hierarchies become unmaintainable and produce subtle bugs when parent internals change. Composition keeps classes independent and testable.
cards.gotcha: The wrapper/decorator pattern (composition) requires forwarding all interface methods — verbose but worth it. Lombok's @Delegate or IDE generation can reduce the boilerplate.

cards.title: Single Responsibility & Open/Closed
cards.explanation: SRP: a class should have only one reason to change — it owns one cohesive responsibility. OCP: classes should be open for extension but closed for modification — add behaviour by adding code, not changing existing code. These two principles work together: SRP keeps classes focused so OCP extensions don't cascade unintended side effects.
cards.whyItMatters: SRP makes classes easier to test and change in isolation. OCP prevents 'ripple effect' — a change in one feature breaking unrelated features.
cards.gotcha: Taken too far, SRP leads to micro-classes with a single method, making the codebase hard to navigate. Aim for cohesion, not atomisation.
